insights: 62.6% of assets are purchased before employee start dates, with a median lead time of 37 days, indicating a strong and systematic pre-hire procurement practice rather than reactive or delayed assignment.

Warranty coverage is fully aligned with employee tenure: 100% of warranties remain active, and median warranty duration covers 7.44 times current median employee tenure (31 days), eliminating any risk of coverage gaps for this cohort.

Procurement timing varies significantly across departments (56-day spread in median purchase-start gaps) and asset categories (80-day spread), with Servers purchased earliest (median -94 days) and Computers latest (median -16 days), revealing structural differences in provisioning logic by segment.

Pre-start procurement shows no cost advantage or penalty: early purchases (>30 days before start) have statistically indistinguishable average costs from on/after-start purchases (p=0.7394, effect size negligible), confirming timing does not drive procurement cost efficiency.

Printer assets show the highest rate of pre-start procurement (76.7%), while Development department shows the lowest (50% before start, median gap only -2 days), highlighting operational divergence in provisioning discipline across functions.

All procurement occurs tightly around hire time—no assets were purchased more than 6 days after start date and none beyond 1 year—confirming that asset assignment is operationally synchronized with onboarding, not decoupled or deferred.
summary: The analysis conclusively refutes the assumed misalignment between asset procurement timing, warranty coverage, and employee tenure: warranty coverage is fully adequate (100% active, 7.44x median tenure coverage), and procurement is tightly clustered around hire time—with two-thirds occurring pre-start and none meaningfully delayed. While significant variation exists across departments and asset categories (e.g., Servers procured 94 days pre-start vs. Computers just 16 days), this variation reflects deliberate provisioning logic rather than dysfunction, and it carries no measurable cost impact. Given the absence of coverage risk or cost leverage from timing adjustments, optimization efforts should shift from calendar-based procurement rules to segment-specific provisioning standards—particularly reinforcing early procurement discipline in low-coverage areas like Development and Computer categories—while maintaining current warranty strategy as optimal.
